activities.shortDescription: Define and plan how to invest in your own professional development
activities.objective: Build deliberate investment in own professional growth
activities.motive: Investing in your own development is the highest-return investment a professional can make

task.whatToDo: Define how you'll invest your professional development budget (company-provided or personal) for the year.
task.howToDo: List priorities: skills to develop, courses, events, books. Allocate budget across them.
guidelines: Mix learning types: books, courses, events
task.suggestedStarter: My professional development plan for this year: [skills] via [resources] with [budget].
task.successCriteria: You created a development plan and spent at least 50% of your budget by mid-year.

activities.reflectionPrompt: Which investment had the highest return on your time and money?

flashcards.title: Principle
flashcards.content: Professional development isn't an expense — it's an investment in your earning and effectiveness.

activities.title: Build a Health and Sustainability Routine
activities.shortDescription: Design a sustainable routine that protects your professional performance over the long term
activities.objective: Build the physical and mental infrastructure for sustained professional performance
activities.motive: Professional performance is unsustainable without physical and mental health systems

task.whatToDo: Design a weekly routine that includes sleep, movement, recovery, and nutrition as non-negotiables.
task.howToDo: Schedule them like meetings. Track for 4 weeks. Adjust based on performance impact.
guidelines: Treat health habits as professional commitments
task.suggestedStarter: My sustainability routine: Sleep [hours] / Movement [days] / Recovery [practices].
task.successCriteria: You maintained your designed health routine for 4+ consecutive weeks.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What was the impact on your professional energy and output?

flashcards.title: Truth
flashcards.content: Professional performance degrades when the person performing isn't maintained.

activities.title: Set Up a Peer Accountability Partnership
activities.shortDescription: Establish a structured accountability relationship with a peer professional
activities.objective: Build external accountability structures for sustained professional performance
activities.motive: Accountability to another person is far more powerful than self-accountability alone

task.whatToDo: Find a peer and set up a formal accountability partnership: weekly check-ins on goals and commitments.
task.howToDo: Share your weekly goals each Monday. Report on progress each Friday. Hold each other to commitments.
task.suggestedStarter: I'm looking for an accountability partner — weekly check-ins on goals. Want to try it?
task.successCriteria: You maintained the accountability partnership for 6+ consecutive weeks.

activities.reflectionPrompt: Did external accountability change what you actually accomplished?

flashcards.title: Why It Works
flashcards.content: Commitment to another person is harder to abandon than commitment to yourself.

activities.title: Build a Network of Trusted Advisors
activities.shortDescription: Deliberately cultivate a board of advisors for your own career
activities.objective: Build a structured support system for long-term career navigation
activities.motive: Complex career decisions are best made with diverse, trusted perspective

task.whatToDo: Identify 3–5 people with different perspectives who can serve as informal advisors for your career.
task.howToDo: Engage each quarterly. Bring specific decisions or challenges. Value their input.
guidelines: Follow up on what you did with their advice
task.suggestedStarter: I'm building a small group of advisors for my career — would you be willing to be one?
task.successCriteria: You identified your 5 advisors and held at least one session with each.

activities.reflectionPrompt: Which advisor's perspective was most different from your own — and most valuable?

flashcards.title: Diversity
flashcards.content: The best advisory boards have people who think nothing like you.

activities.title: Build a Personal Brand Inside Your Organization
activities.shortDescription: Define and deliberately shape how you're perceived professionally
activities.objective: Build deliberate professional reputation management
activities.motive: Your professional brand exists whether you manage it or not — managing it is better

task.whatToDo: Define your professional brand: what you want to be known for, and design 3 actions to reinforce it.
task.howToDo: Ask: What do I want to be known for? Who needs to know it? What do I need to do to demonstrate it?
guidelines: Ask for feedback on your current perception
task.suggestedStarter: I want to be known for [X]. Here's how I'm going to demonstrate it.
task.successCriteria: You defined your brand and took 3 concrete actions to reinforce it over 4 weeks.

activities.reflectionPrompt: How aligned is how others perceive you with how you want to be perceived?

flashcards.title: Brand
flashcards.content: Your brand is what people say about you when you're not in the room.

activities.title: Publish a Professional Point of View Quarterly
activities.shortDescription: Write and share a professional perspective piece every quarter
activities.objective: Build consistent thought leadership and professional visibility
activities.motive: Regular published perspective compounds professional credibility over time

task.whatToDo: Commit to writing one professional article or long-form post each quarter.
task.howToDo: Share it internally and/or externally. Track engagement and refine your approach.
task.suggestedStarter: Each quarter, I publish one piece on [domain] — this is the habit that compounds reputation.
task.successCriteria: You published 4 pieces in 12 months and grew your professional audience.

activities.reflectionPrompt: Which piece got the most response? Why?

flashcards.title: Compound
flashcards.content: 4 pieces a year over 5 years = 20 pieces. That's a body of professional work.

activities.title: Build a Personal Operating Manual
activities.shortDescription: Document how you work best and share it with your team
activities.objective: Build self-awareness and team clarity through transparent self-documentation
activities.motive: People who know how you work best collaborate with you more effectively

task.whatToDo: Write a 1–2 page 'how I work' document and share it with your team.
task.howToDo: Cover: working style, communication preferences, how to give me feedback, what I value, where I struggle.
guidelines: Invite others to do the same
task.suggestedStarter: I've written a 'how I work' document — here's what I want you to know about working with me.
task.successCriteria: You shared your personal operating manual and at least 2 teammates changed how they worked with you.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What was hardest to admit about how you work?

flashcards.title: What to Include
flashcards.content: Style / Communication preferences / Feedback approach / Values / Where I struggle.

activities.title: Design a Sabbatical or Deep Rest Period
activities.shortDescription: Plan and take a structured period of deliberate recovery and reflection
activities.objective: Build the discipline and infrastructure to sustain professional performance through deliberate rest
activities.motive: High performers who don't rest eventually don't perform. Recovery is a professional strategy.

task.whatToDo: Plan and take a deliberate rest period — a sabbatical, staycation, or unplugged week — and design it intentionally.
task.howToDo: Define what you'll stop doing, what you'll do instead, and what you want to emerge with.
task.suggestedStarter: I'm planning a recovery week — here's what I'll stop doing and what I want from the time.
task.successCriteria: You took a deliberate rest period and documented what changed in your energy and clarity afterward.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What perspective did distance from work create?

flashcards.title: Insight
flashcards.content: The best ideas often arrive when you stop pushing for them.

activities.title: Write a 5-Year Professional Manifesto
activities.shortDescription: Define your professional principles, priorities, and purpose for the next 5 years
activities.objective: Build long-term professional clarity and intentionality
activities.motive: Professionals who know what they stand for make better decisions and build more purposeful careers

task.whatToDo: Write a 1–2 page professional manifesto: what you believe, what you're working toward, and what you won't compromise on.
task.howToDo: Cover: core professional beliefs, 5-year vision, non-negotiable standards, and how you want to be remembered.
guidelines: Let it be aspirational and uncomfortable
task.suggestedStarter: I believe [X]. I'm building toward [Y]. I will not compromise on [Z].
task.successCriteria: You wrote and shared your manifesto with at least one trusted colleague.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What surprised you about what you actually believe?

flashcards.title: Sections
flashcards.content: Beliefs / Vision / Non-negotiables / How I want to be remembered.

activities.title: Identify a Potential Mentor
activities.shortDescription: Name one person you'd want to learn from and why
activities.objective: Begin the process of building a mentoring relationship
activities.motive: You can't seek a mentor you haven't identified

task.whatToDo: Write down the name of one person you'd genuinely learn from and what specifically you'd want to learn.
task.howToDo: Name them, list 3 things you'd want to learn from them, and write why they're the right person.
guidelines: Be specific about what you'd learn
Don't just pick the most senior person
task.suggestedStarter: I'd want to learn from [Name] because they're exceptional at [specific skill/area].
task.successCriteria: You identified one potential mentor and articulated 3 specific things you'd want to learn from them.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What does your ideal mentor have that your current network lacks?

flashcards.title: Clarity
flashcards.content: Identify a mentor by what you want to learn, not who is most impressive.

activities.title: Prepare Questions for a Mentor
activities.shortDescription: Draft specific questions before your first mentoring session
activities.objective: Build the habit of arriving at mentoring sessions prepared
activities.motive: Unprepared mentees waste the mentor's time and their own opportunity

task.whatToDo: Prepare 5 specific questions for an upcoming or hypothetical mentoring conversation.
task.howToDo: Frame questions around specific challenges, decisions, or skills — not just 'what advice do you have?'
guidelines: Include at least one hard question
task.suggestedStarter: What's the thing you wish you'd known when you were in my position about [X]?
task.successCriteria: You prepared 5 specific, non-generic questions for a mentoring conversation.

activities.reflectionPrompt: Which question are you most nervous to ask? That's probably the one to prioritize.

flashcards.title: Rule
flashcards.content: Generic questions: 'What's your advice?' Good questions: 'How did you handle X when Y happened?'

activities.title: Ask Your First Mentor for Time
activities.shortDescription: Reach out and formally request a mentoring session
activities.objective: Practice initiating a mentoring relationship
activities.motive: Mentors don't find mentees. Mentees have to initiate.

task.whatToDo: Reach out to your identified potential mentor and request a 30-minute meeting.
task.howToDo: Send a concise message explaining who you are, why you're reaching out to them specifically, and what you'd like to discuss.
guidelines: Make the time request easy to say yes to
task.suggestedStarter: Hi [Name], I'm [brief context]. I'd love 30 minutes to learn from your experience in [X].
task.successCriteria: You sent the outreach message and received a response.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What made you hesitate to send it?

flashcards.title: Outreach Formula
flashcards.content: Who you are + Why them specifically + Specific ask = a message worth responding to.

activities.title: Take Notes During a Mentoring Session
activities.shortDescription: Document key takeaways from every mentoring conversation
activities.objective: Build the habit of extracting and retaining mentoring insights
activities.motive: A conversation without notes is a conversation mostly forgotten

task.whatToDo: During your next mentoring session, take structured notes and write a summary within 24 hours.
task.howToDo: Note: key insights, advice, follow-up actions, and one thing you'll do differently.
guidelines: Focus on insights, not just facts
Act on at least one thing within a week
task.suggestedStarter: Key insights from [Name] session: / Actions I'll take: / Questions for next time:
task.successCriteria: You wrote a session summary within 24 hours and took at least one documented action.

activities.reflectionPrompt: Which piece of advice will most change how you work?

flashcards.title: Three Things
flashcards.content: Key insights / Actions / Questions for next session.

activities.title: Share Your Progress With Your Mentor
activities.shortDescription: Update your mentor on what you've done with their advice
activities.objective: Build accountability and reciprocity in mentoring relationships
activities.motive: Showing a mentor you acted on their advice earns the relationship and more advice

task.whatToDo: Send a follow-up message to your mentor telling them what you did with their advice.
task.howToDo: Reference their specific advice, what you did, and what happened.
guidelines: This isn't a thank-you note — it's a progress update
task.suggestedStarter: You suggested I try [X] — I did it, and here's what happened: [Y].
task.successCriteria: You sent a specific progress update that referenced the mentor's advice and your action.

activities.reflectionPrompt: How did your mentor respond to learning you'd acted on their advice?

flashcards.title: Reciprocity
flashcards.content: Acting on advice and reporting back is what earns the next round of advice.

activities.title: Mentor Someone Below You
activities.shortDescription: Proactively offer support to someone more junior than yourself
leadershipPrinciples: Success and Scale Bring Broad Responsibility
activities.objective: Begin building mentoring skills from the giving side
activities.motive: You don't need to be senior to mentor. You just need to be a step ahead.

task.whatToDo: Identify someone junior to you and offer to share your experience in one area they're navigating.
task.howToDo: Reach out, schedule a session, listen to their challenge, and share relevant experience.
guidelines: Share what you learned, not just what to do
task.suggestedStarter: I noticed you're working on [X] — I went through something similar and might have some useful perspective.
task.successCriteria: You completed a mentoring session with a junior colleague and they took at least one action from it.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What did you learn about your own experience by trying to teach it?

flashcards.title: Rule
flashcards.content: You're ready to mentor the moment you've learned something someone else hasn't yet.

activities.title: Ask Your Mentor to Challenge You
activities.shortDescription: Request direct, challenging feedback rather than just support
activities.objective: Develop the ability to seek and receive challenging feedback
activities.motive: Mentors who only encourage aren't developing you

task.whatToDo: Explicitly ask your mentor to challenge your thinking, not just validate it.
task.howToDo: Ask: 'Where do you think I'm wrong? What blind spot do you see in how I'm approaching this?'
guidelines: Be genuinely open to being wrong
task.suggestedStarter: I'd like you to push back on my thinking here — what am I not seeing?
task.successCriteria: Your mentor challenged you, and you engaged with the challenge rather than defending.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What assumption did your mentor challenge that you hadn't examined?

flashcards.title: Signal
flashcards.content: A good mentor makes you uncomfortable in useful ways.

activities.title: Create a Mentoring Agenda
activities.shortDescription: Build a structured agenda for a recurring mentoring relationship
activities.objective: Build structure into a mentoring relationship for better outcomes
activities.motive: Unstructured mentoring conversations feel great but often produce little

task.whatToDo: Design a session-by-session agenda for your next 3 mentoring sessions.
task.howToDo: For each session: one topic, one challenge to discuss, one question to ask, one action to report.
guidelines: Send the agenda before the session
Stick to one topic per session
task.suggestedStarter: Session 1: Topic [X] / Challenge to discuss: [Y] / Action to report: [Z].
task.successCriteria: You ran 3 mentoring sessions with a prepared agenda and produced 3 documented actions.

activities.reflectionPrompt: Did structure make the sessions more or less valuable?

flashcards.title: Per-Session Format
flashcards.content: Topic / Challenge / Question / Action to report.

activities.title: Build a Reverse Mentoring Relationship
activities.shortDescription: Ask a more junior colleague to teach you something they know
activities.objective: Develop learning humility by seeking expertise from junior professionals
activities.motive: Expertise doesn't always correlate with seniority

task.whatToDo: Identify something a more junior colleague knows better than you and ask them to teach you.
task.howToDo: Set up a session. Come to learn, not to evaluate. Take notes.
task.suggestedStarter: You know [X] much better than I do — would you teach me?
task.successCriteria: You completed a reverse mentoring session and applied at least one thing you learned.

activities.reflectionPrompt: How did being in the 'learner' role change your perspective on your juniors?

flashcards.title: Truth
flashcards.content: Expertise doesn't flow only from senior to junior. Great leaders learn from everyone.

activities.title: Document Lessons From Your Mentoring
activities.shortDescription: Compile insights from your mentoring sessions into a shareable document
leadershipPrinciples: Success and Scale Bring Broad Responsibility
activities.objective: Compound the value of mentoring by capturing and sharing insights
activities.motive: Lessons captured become teachable. Lessons lost stay personal.

task.whatToDo: Compile your key mentoring insights into a 1–2 page document and share it with one person.
task.howToDo: Group insights by theme: career, communication, leadership, technical, relationships.
guidelines: Focus on insights that changed your thinking
Share with a specific person who'd benefit
task.suggestedStarter: Things I've learned from mentors that changed how I work: ...
task.successCriteria: You created and shared a compiled mentoring insights document.

activities.reflectionPrompt: Which insight had the most impact on your actual behavior?

flashcards.title: Why Document
flashcards.content: Lessons captured can be shared. Shared lessons multiply in value.

activities.title: Shadow a Senior Professional for a Day
activities.shortDescription: Spend a day observing how a senior colleague works
activities.objective: Learn through direct observation of experienced professional practice
activities.motive: Watching how senior professionals actually work reveals what advice can't

task.whatToDo: Request to shadow a senior professional for a full workday.
task.howToDo: Observe, take notes, and ask 3 specific questions at the end about things you noticed.
task.suggestedStarter: I'd love to shadow you for a day — I'm trying to understand how [role/skill] works in practice.
task.successCriteria: You shadowed for the day and produced 3 specific observations that changed how you think.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What gap did you notice between your expectation of the role and the reality?

flashcards.title: Focus
flashcards.content: Observe decisions and reactions — that's where expertise shows most clearly.

activities.title: Give Your Mentee a Real Challenge
activities.shortDescription: Assign a stretch challenge to someone you're mentoring
leadershipPrinciples: Success and Scale Bring Broad Responsibility
activities.objective: Build skill in developing others through stretch assignments
activities.motive: The most effective mentors challenge, not just encourage

task.whatToDo: Assign a stretch challenge to someone you're mentoring — something beyond their comfort zone.
task.howToDo: Frame the challenge, explain why you believe they can do it, and schedule a debrief.
task.suggestedStarter: I think you're ready for something harder — here's a challenge I'd like to give you.
task.successCriteria: Your mentee attempted the challenge and you debriefed together on what they learned.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What did the challenge reveal about your mentee's potential?

flashcards.title: Development
flashcards.content: Encouragement develops confidence. Challenge develops capability.

activities.title: Build a Mentoring Plan for Your Mentee
activities.shortDescription: Design a structured development plan for someone you're mentoring
leadershipPrinciples: Success and Scale Bring Broad Responsibility
activities.objective: Build structured mentoring capability
activities.motive: Mentoring without a plan is friendship. Mentoring with a plan is development.

task.whatToDo: Co-create a 90-day development plan with your mentee.
task.howToDo: Define: 3 goals, specific actions for each, accountability checkpoints, and how progress will be measured.
task.suggestedStarter: Let's design your next 90 days together — what do you most want to grow in?
task.successCriteria: You completed the plan and reviewed progress at 30, 60, and 90 days.

activities.reflectionPrompt: Where did your mentee surprise you?

flashcards.title: Structure
flashcards.content: 3 goals / Specific actions / Accountability checkpoints / Measurement.

activities.title: Have a Difficult Mentoring Conversation
activities.shortDescription: Tell your mentee something hard that they need to hear
activities.objective: Build courage in delivering difficult but necessary mentoring feedback
activities.motive: Mentors who only say comfortable things aren't doing their job

task.whatToDo: Identify one thing your mentee needs to hear that they probably won't enjoy hearing and say it.
task.howToDo: Frame with care, but don't soften it to meaninglessness. Be specific. Offer a path forward.
guidelines: End with a clear path forward
task.suggestedStarter: I want to tell you something that I think matters for your growth — and it might be hard to hear.
task.successCriteria: You delivered the difficult message and your mentee engaged with it constructively.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What happened after you said the hard thing?

flashcards.title: Mentor Duty
flashcards.content: The kindest thing a mentor can do is tell the truth — even when it's uncomfortable.

activities.title: Facilitate a Group Mentoring Session
activities.shortDescription: Run a mentoring session with a small group of junior professionals
leadershipPrinciples: Success and Scale Bring Broad Responsibility
activities.objective: Scale mentoring impact by working with groups
activities.motive: Group mentoring multiplies your impact without multiplying your time

task.whatToDo: Facilitate a group mentoring session with 3–5 junior professionals on a shared topic.
task.howToDo: Prepare 3 teaching points. Facilitate discussion. Allow peer-to-peer sharing. End with Q&A.
task.suggestedStarter: Today I'll share 3 things I wish I'd known earlier — then I'd love your questions and perspectives.
task.successCriteria: You facilitated a group mentoring session and all participants named one specific takeaway.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What did the group dynamic reveal that 1:1 sessions wouldn't have?

flashcards.title: Leverage
flashcards.content: Group mentoring scales your impact without scaling your time.

activities.title: Ask Your Mentor About Their Failures
activities.shortDescription: Use a mentoring session specifically to learn from your mentor's mistakes
activities.objective: Extract more honest and useful learning from mentoring relationships
activities.motive: Mentors' failures are often more educational than their successes

task.whatToDo: Ask your mentor to share a significant professional failure and what they learned from it.
task.howToDo: Prepare 3–4 follow-up questions to go deeper on the story and the lesson.
guidelines: Go deep — don't accept a surface story
Connect the lesson to your own situation
task.suggestedStarter: What's a significant failure you've had that you think I should learn from before experiencing it myself?
task.successCriteria: You heard a genuine failure story and drew a specific lesson applicable to your current work.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What failure did you narrowly avoid because of this conversation?

flashcards.title: Lesson Source
flashcards.content: Failures are often more instructive than successes. Ask about them.

activities.title: Create a Mentoring Network
activities.shortDescription: Build relationships with multiple mentors across different domains
activities.objective: Build a diversified mentoring portfolio
activities.motive: One mentor has one perspective. Multiple mentors have many.

task.whatToDo: Identify and develop 3 mentoring relationships across different expertise domains.
task.howToDo: One for domain expertise, one for career navigation, one for leadership development.
task.suggestedStarter: I'm building a network of mentors across different areas — here are the gaps I'm filling.
task.successCriteria: You held at least 2 sessions with each of your 3 mentors within 3 months.

activities.reflectionPrompt: How did the perspectives from different mentors complement or contradict each other?

flashcards.title: Portfolio
flashcards.content: Domain expertise / Career navigation / Leadership — one mentor for each.

activities.title: Develop a Teaching Curriculum from Your Experience
activities.shortDescription: Turn your professional experience into structured learning for others
leadershipPrinciples: Success and Scale Bring Broad Responsibility
activities.objective: Institutionalize your expertise for the benefit of others
activities.motive: Expertise is most valuable when it can be transferred

task.whatToDo: Design a 3–5 session curriculum from your professional experience on one topic you know well.
task.howToDo: Session 1: Foundation. Sessions 2–4: Core skills. Session 5: Advanced application.
guidelines: Teach what you wish you'd known
Build in practice, not just theory
task.suggestedStarter: Based on my experience in [domain], here's the curriculum I'd use to develop someone new to it.
task.successCriteria: You designed the curriculum and delivered at least the first session.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What was hardest to turn from personal experience into teachable content?

flashcards.title: Curriculum Shape
flashcards.content: Foundation → Core skills (× 3) → Advanced application.

activities.title: Sponsor a Mentee for a Visible Opportunity
activities.shortDescription: Move from mentoring to sponsorship — use your capital for your mentee
leadershipPrinciples: Success and Scale Bring Broad Responsibility
activities.objective: Develop the ability to sponsor others for professional advancement
activities.motive: Mentorship changes how someone thinks. Sponsorship changes what's available to them.

task.whatToDo: When a visible opportunity arises — speaking, leading, presenting — nominate your mentee for it.
task.howToDo: Make a specific, reasoned recommendation on their behalf using your reputation.
guidelines: Only sponsor people you're genuinely confident in
task.suggestedStarter: I'd like to recommend [Name] for [opportunity] — here's why they're ready.
task.successCriteria: Your mentee received the opportunity through your sponsorship and executed it successfully.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What did it feel like to spend your professional capital on someone else?

flashcards.title: Distinction
flashcards.content: Mentors give advice. Sponsors give opportunities.

activities.title: Lead a Workshop Based on Your Expertise
activities.shortDescription: Design and deliver a hands-on workshop for a group of learners
activities.objective: Scale mentoring through structured group learning
activities.motive: A workshop reaches more people and creates more lasting learning than individual sessions

task.whatToDo: Design and deliver a 2-hour workshop on a topic you know well.
task.howToDo: Include: framing, teaching, practice activity, and debrief. Limit lecture to 40% of the session.
task.suggestedStarter: I'm running a 2-hour workshop on [topic] — 60% of the time will be hands-on.
task.successCriteria: You delivered the workshop and participants rated it 4+/5 on average.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What part of the workshop produced the most learning?

flashcards.title: Rule
flashcards.content: Lecture ≤ 40%. Doing ≥ 60%. Learning happens in the doing.

activities.title: Give a Formal Endorsement in a Performance System
activities.shortDescription: Provide a detailed performance endorsement for your mentee
activities.objective: Build the skill of formal advocacy within performance systems
activities.motive: Your mentee's growth inside the organization depends partly on how well you represent them

task.whatToDo: Write a detailed performance endorsement for your mentee during a review cycle.
task.howToDo: Include specific examples, outcomes, and growth evidence — not generic praise.
task.suggestedStarter: [Name] demonstrated [skill] when they [specific example] and the result was [outcome].
task.successCriteria: You wrote a detailed endorsement that included at least 3 specific examples with outcomes.

activities.reflectionPrompt: Did your endorsement change the outcome for your mentee?

flashcards.title: Endorsement Formula
flashcards.content: Skill demonstrated + Specific example + Measurable outcome.

activities.title: Run a Mentoring Circle
activities.shortDescription: Facilitate a regular peer mentoring circle for 4–6 professionals
leadershipPrinciples: Success and Scale Bring Broad Responsibility
activities.objective: Scale mentoring impact through structured peer learning communities
activities.motive: A circle of peers learning together produces more insight than any single mentor can provide

task.whatToDo: Launch and facilitate a monthly mentoring circle for 4–6 professionals.
task.howToDo: Each session: one person brings a challenge. Others ask questions and share perspective. Rotate leadership.
task.suggestedStarter: I'm starting a monthly mentoring circle — each session, one person brings a real challenge.
task.successCriteria: You ran 4+ monthly circles with consistent participation from all members.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What challenge presented by others gave you the most useful mirror into your own work?

flashcards.title: Circle Format
flashcards.content: One person's challenge → Questions from group → Shared perspectives → Action.

activities.title: Build a Formal Mentoring Program
activities.shortDescription: Design and launch a structured mentoring program for your organization
leadershipPrinciples: Success and Scale Bring Broad Responsibility
activities.objective: Scale mentoring across an organization through program design
activities.motive: Individual mentoring helps one person. A program helps many.

task.whatToDo: Design and launch a formal mentoring program for your team, department, or organization.
task.howToDo: Define: matching criteria, session format, program duration, training for mentors, and success metrics.
guidelines: Train the mentors, not just the mentees
task.suggestedStarter: I want to design a mentoring program that actually develops people — here's my proposal.
task.successCriteria: You launched the program with at least 5 mentor-mentee pairs and tracked 3 months of outcomes.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What would you change in the design based on the first cohort's experience?

flashcards.title: Program Design
flashcards.content: Matching + Format + Duration + Mentor training + Outcome metrics.

activities.title: Write a Career Development Playbook
activities.shortDescription: Document the career development insights you've gathered into a shareable guide
leadershipPrinciples: Success and Scale Bring Broad Responsibility
activities.objective: Scale your mentoring impact through written resources
activities.motive: Written guidance reaches more people than any individual can mentor

task.whatToDo: Write a practical career development playbook for professionals in your domain.
task.howToDo: Cover: key skills, common mistakes, how to navigate growth stages, and resources.
task.suggestedStarter: This is what I wish I'd had when I was starting in [domain] — a practical guide to what actually matters.
task.successCriteria: You published the playbook and at least 3 professionals referenced it as useful.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What was the hardest thing to put in writing?

flashcards.title: Sections
flashcards.content: Key skills / Common mistakes / Growth stages / Resources.

activities.title: Coach Someone Through a Major Decision
activities.shortDescription: Guide a mentee through a complex career or work decision using coaching techniques
activities.objective: Build coaching skills alongside mentoring skills
activities.motive: Coaching empowers people to find their own answers. Mentoring gives them yours.

task.whatToDo: Guide a mentee through a major decision using coaching questions — not your recommendations.
task.howToDo: Use: What are the options? What matters most to you? What would you advise a friend to do? What's the cost of inaction?
guidelines: Don't give your recommendation until asked
task.suggestedStarter: Tell me more about what you're weighing. What's the option you keep coming back to, and why?
task.successCriteria: Your mentee reached and committed to a decision, crediting the process — not your advice.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What was hardest about not giving your own opinion?

flashcards.title: Coaching vs. Mentoring
flashcards.content: Mentoring: 'Here's what I'd do.' Coaching: 'What do you think you should do?'

activities.title: Create a Succession Path for Your Mentee
activities.shortDescription: Design a structured path for your mentee to step into a more senior role
leadershipPrinciples: Success and Scale Bring Broad Responsibility
activities.objective: Build the ability to develop talent to the point of succession
activities.motive: The ultimate goal of mentoring is making yourself unnecessary to your mentee

task.whatToDo: Create a structured path to a specific role for your mentee, with milestones and your direct support.
task.howToDo: Define the target role, the gaps, the milestones, and what you'll specifically do to enable each stage.
guidelines: Step back progressively as they grow
task.suggestedStarter: I want to prepare you for [role] — here's what I think it will take and how I'll support you.
task.successCriteria: Your mentee progressed to the next level within 12 months, partly through your structured support.

activities.reflectionPrompt: At what point did your mentee no longer need your guidance?

flashcards.title: Goal
flashcards.content: Succession means your mentee outgrows their need for you.

flashcards.title: Approach
flashcards.content: Target role → Gaps → Milestones → Your support → Progressive withdrawal.

activities.title: Mentor Someone from a Different Function or Industry
activities.shortDescription: Expand your mentoring reach outside your expertise area
leadershipPrinciples: Success and Scale Bring Broad Responsibility
activities.objective: Develop mentoring skills that transcend domain knowledge
activities.motive: Cross-functional mentoring teaches you as much as it teaches them

task.whatToDo: Take on a mentee from a different function, industry, or domain than your expertise.
task.howToDo: Focus on universal skills: decision-making, communication, resilience. Learn their domain alongside mentoring.
task.suggestedStarter: I know your domain better than I do, but I can help you think through career strategy — what matters to you most?
task.successCriteria: Your mentee credits you with meaningful growth while you gained insights into their industry.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What did their domain teach you that you wouldn't have learned otherwise?

flashcards.title: Insight
flashcards.content: The best mentoring transcends the mentor's expertise.

flashcards.title: Skill
flashcards.content: Ask, listen, share universal principles, learn together.

activities.title: Mentor Someone Through Career Failure or Setback
activities.shortDescription: Guide a mentee who has experienced significant failure or career stagnation
activities.objective: Build mentoring resilience and help others navigate failure
activities.motive: Mentoring at the moment of failure shapes someone's entire career trajectory

task.whatToDo: Be the steady presence for someone navigating a major setback: job loss, missed promotion, failed project, or public failure.
task.howToDo: Help them separate identity from outcome, extract lessons, and rebuild momentum. Share your own failures.
task.suggestedStarter: This is hard. You're not the first good person this has happened to. Let's figure out what comes next.
task.successCriteria: Your mentee moved past blame, extracted key lessons, and took meaningful action toward recovery.

activities.reflectionPrompt: How did supporting them through failure change your relationship?

flashcards.title: Principle
flashcards.content: How you mentor through failure is how they'll mentor others someday.

flashcards.title: Approach
flashcards.content: Validate → Lessons → Agency → Action.

activities.title: Build a Mentoring Culture in Your Organization
activities.shortDescription: Establish mentoring as a core organizational practice
leadershipPrinciples: Success and Scale Bring Broad Responsibility
activities.objective: Scale mentoring impact across an entire organization
activities.motive: Organizational culture compounds the impact of individual mentoring

task.whatToDo: Lead an organizational initiative to make mentoring a standard practice, not an exception.
task.howToDo: Develop: mentoring norms, training for mentors, matching systems, dedicated time, recognition, and metrics.
guidelines: Make it easy to say yes
task.suggestedStarter: I want every person here to have a mentor. Here's how we make that happen and sustain it.
task.successCriteria: At least 60% of your organization is in active mentor-mentee relationships after 12 months.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What barriers to mentoring did you eliminate, and which proved hardest to overcome?

flashcards.title: Culture Shift
flashcards.content: Mentoring goes from 'nice to have' to 'how we develop people here.'

flashcards.title: Levers
flashcards.content: Norms + Training + Matching + Time + Recognition + Metrics.

activities.title: Mentor Someone You Fundamentally Disagree With
activities.shortDescription: Develop a mentoring relationship across significant ideological, professional, or personal differences
activities.objective: Master mentoring across deep differences
activities.motive: True mentoring doesn't require agreement — it requires seeing potential in someone whose worldview differs from yours

task.whatToDo: Take on a mentee whose approach, values, or perspectives differ significantly from yours. Mentor them with integrity.
task.howToDo: Separate development from agreement. Focus on their growth, not changing their mind. Model respectful disagreement.
guidelines: Find common ground in principles, not conclusions
task.suggestedStarter: We see this differently, and I respect your thinking. Here's how I'd approach it — and why your approach has merit too.
task.successCriteria: Your mentee grew significantly, credited you with their development, and respects your differences rather than hiding them.

activities.reflectionPrompt: How did being disagreed with become a source of growth for you?

flashcards.title: Mastery
flashcards.content: The hardest mentoring is across the deepest disagreements.

flashcards.title: Principle
flashcards.content: You can mentor someone you don't fully understand — that's often when mentoring matters most.

activities.title: Read One Article on Your Field
activities.shortDescription: Consume professional knowledge for 20 minutes
activities.objective: Establish a habit of regular professional reading
activities.motive: Learning compounds. Small consistent actions build expertise

task.whatToDo: Find and read one article, blog post, or research summary relevant to your field.
task.howToDo: Search industry publications, LinkedIn, or newsletters. Read carefully. Note one key takeaway.
task.suggestedStarter: Check [industry publication] for this week's trending topics.
task.successCriteria: You read one full article and wrote down one actionable insight.

activities.reflectionPrompt: How does this article change how you'll approach your work this week?

flashcards.title: Habit
flashcards.content: Professionals read daily. Start with one article.

flashcards.title: Source
flashcards.content: Industry publications > random blogs > listicles.

activities.title: Watch an Educational Video on a New Tool
activities.shortDescription: Learn a specific skill through video instruction
activities.objective: Build toolkit awareness across your domain
activities.motive: Tools amplify what you can do

task.whatToDo: Watch a tutorial on a tool, platform, or framework you've heard about but don't fully understand.
task.howToDo: Find a 30-45 minute tutorial. Watch actively. Try the basics yourself immediately after.
guidelines: Follow along with your own project
task.suggestedStarter: I keep hearing about [tool] — I'll watch a tutorial and try it on my next project.
task.successCriteria: You completed the tutorial and used the tool for at least one real task.

activities.reflectionPrompt: When will you actually use this tool in your work?

flashcards.title: Learning
flashcards.content: Passive watching teaches nothing. You must practice immediately.

flashcards.title: Tool
flashcards.content: Learn tools when you have a real problem to solve.

activities.title: Complete One Online Course Module
activities.shortDescription: Finish a structured lesson from an online learning platform
activities.objective: Progress through structured professional development
activities.motive: Structured learning builds depth faster than random reading

task.whatToDo: Enroll in a course relevant to your role and complete one full module.
task.howToDo: Choose platforms like Coursera, LinkedIn Learning, or Udacity. Pick a course that solves a real gap.
guidelines: Finish the entire module, not just skim it
task.suggestedStarter: I need to understand [skill] better — I'll take the [Platform] course on it.
task.successCriteria: You completed the module and passed any assessment with 80%+.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What's one concept you'll apply at work this week?

flashcards.title: Depth
flashcards.content: Structured courses go deeper than articles. Use them for real gaps.

flashcards.title: Accountability
flashcards.content: Quizzes force actual learning, not just reading.

activities.title: Attend a Conference Talk or Webinar
activities.shortDescription: Learn from industry experts in a live or recorded session
activities.objective: Expose yourself to cutting-edge thinking in your field
activities.motive: Conferences accelerate learning through concentrated expertise

task.whatToDo: Register for and attend a conference talk, webinar, or expert panel in your field.
task.howToDo: Find relevant conferences or webinar series. Attend live or watch recordings. Take structured notes.
guidelines: Go in with 2–3 questions you want answered
task.suggestedStarter: I want to understand [topic] better. I'll attend [conference/webinar] on it.
task.successCriteria: You attended, took notes, and had one conversation with another attendee.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What's the most surprising thing you learned that contradicts what you believed?

flashcards.title: Value
flashcards.content: Conferences expose gaps in your thinking. Pay attention to the surprises.

flashcards.title: Action
flashcards.content: Attend with intent. Know what you want to learn.

activities.title: Deep Dive into One Concept
activities.shortDescription: Spend focused time understanding a complex idea
activities.objective: Develop the discipline to understand complex topics thoroughly
activities.motive: Most people skim. Mastery comes from going deep

task.whatToDo: Choose one concept central to your role that you half-understand. Become expert-level knowledgeable.
task.howToDo: Read multiple sources (article, research paper, expert explanation). Create a document explaining it to a beginner.
guidelines: Don't move on until you can explain it clearly
task.suggestedStarter: I think I understand [concept], but I want to really master it. Here's what I'm learning...
task.successCriteria: You explained the concept to a colleague clearly, and they said it made sense.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What did you misunderstand that you now see clearly?

flashcards.title: Mastery
flashcards.content: You understand something when you can explain it to someone else.

flashcards.title: Approach
flashcards.content: Multiple sources + Explanation + Teaching = Mastery.

activities.title: Join a Professional Community or Slack Group
activities.shortDescription: Become an active member of a learning community
activities.objective: Embed yourself in a continuous learning environment
activities.motive: Communities provide accountability, fresh perspectives, and serendipitous learning

task.whatToDo: Join an active online community in your field (Slack group, Discord, Reddit, community forum).
task.howToDo: Introduce yourself. Spend 20–30 minutes weekly reading discussions. Ask one meaningful question in week one.
task.suggestedStarter: I'm new here — I'm a [role] at [company] working on [domain]. What should I know?
task.successCriteria: You're an active member after 4 weeks, with at least 3 meaningful contributions.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What did you learn from the community that you wouldn't have found alone?

flashcards.title: Value
flashcards.content: Communities keep you current and connected to peers.

flashcards.title: Participation
flashcards.content: Observe first, then contribute. Quality over quantity.

activities.title: Reverse-Mentor Someone on Modern Tools or Trends
activities.shortDescription: Teach someone senior about new technology or approaches
leadershipPrinciples: Success and Scale Bring Broad Responsibility
activities.objective: Develop teaching skills while staying current with emerging trends
activities.motive: Teaching deepens your understanding

task.whatToDo: Find someone senior who wants to learn about an emerging tool or trend you understand.
task.howToDo: Explain the basics. Show how it works. Relate it to their experience. Offer to help them experiment.
guidelines: Focus on why it matters, not how
task.suggestedStarter: I've noticed you'd probably benefit from understanding [trend] — I'd love to walk you through it.
task.successCriteria: Your mentee is now using or seriously exploring the tool/trend you taught them.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What did teaching them teach you?

flashcards.title: Insight
flashcards.content: Teaching forces you to truly understand something.

flashcards.title: Value
flashcards.content: Reverse mentoring keeps senior people current while valuing your expertise.

activities.title: Create a Personal Learning Plan for One Quarter
activities.shortDescription: Design a structured approach to your professional growth
activities.objective: Move learning from reactive to strategic
activities.motive: Strategic learning compounds. Reactive learning keeps you busy

task.whatToDo: Document a 90-day learning plan addressing 3 skill gaps relevant to your next role or growth.
task.howToDo: Identify gaps. Select resources (courses, books, mentors). Schedule time. Plan how you'll apply what you learn.
guidelines: Be specific about the skill, not vague
task.suggestedStarter: To move into [role], I need to grow in [skills]. Here's my 90-day plan...
task.successCriteria: You completed 70%+ of your planned learning and applied at least 3 lessons at work.

activities.reflectionPrompt: Which learning source was most valuable, and why?

flashcards.title: Strategy
flashcards.content: Strategic learning: Identify gap → Plan → Execute → Apply.

flashcards.title: Goal
flashcards.content: Learning isn't about completion certificates. It's about capability change.

activities.title: Write a Blog Post or Internal Article Explaining Something You Learned
activities.shortDescription: Share knowledge through written teaching
leadershipPrinciples: Success and Scale Bring Broad Responsibility
activities.objective: Deepen learning through teaching and scale your knowledge
activities.motive: Writing forces clarity. Publishing forces accountability

task.whatToDo: Write a 1000–1500 word article or internal doc explaining a concept, process, or skill you recently mastered.
task.howToDo: Choose something you learned this quarter. Explain it like you're teaching a peer. Include examples and mistakes to avoid.
task.suggestedStarter: I learned [concept] and spent weeks understanding it. Here's what actually matters...
task.successCriteria: You published it and at least 3 people referenced it as helpful.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What became clear only when you had to explain it in writing?

flashcards.title: Principle
flashcards.content: If you can't write it clearly, you don't understand it clearly.

flashcards.title: Impact
flashcards.content: Your learning helps you. Your writing helps your whole organization.

activities.title: Complete a Technical or Professional Certification
activities.shortDescription: Earn a credential that validates specific expertise
activities.objective: Develop depth in a critical capability with external validation
activities.motive: Certifications force comprehensive learning and provide external credibility

task.whatToDo: Complete a certification exam in a skill critical to your role or next opportunity.
task.howToDo: Study systematically. Use official materials. Take practice exams. Set a test date to maintain momentum.
guidelines: Pass on first or second attempt
task.suggestedStarter: I'm pursuing the [Certification] because it'll prepare me for [opportunity].
task.successCriteria: You passed the certification exam and added it to your credentials.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What surprised you about how deep this topic actually goes?

flashcards.title: Value
flashcards.content: Certifications force depth and provide external proof.

flashcards.title: ROI
flashcards.content: Choose certifications that matter in your industry, not easy ones.

activities.title: Learn From Mistakes: Document a Failure
activities.shortDescription: Systematically extract learning from something that didn't work
activities.objective: Turn failure into systematic learning
activities.motive: Mistakes are the most efficient teacher if you extract the lessons

task.whatToDo: Document a recent mistake or failure at work. Extract the lesson and the behavioral change it demands.
task.howToDo: What happened? Why did you miss it? What would you do differently? What's your early warning system next time?
task.suggestedStarter: I didn't catch [mistake] because I [root cause]. Next time, I'll [specific change].
task.successCriteria: You documented one lesson and applied one behavioral change based on it.

activities.reflectionPrompt: How will you remember this lesson a year from now?

flashcards.title: Learning
flashcards.content: Fast learning means extracting lessons from small mistakes before they become big ones.

flashcards.title: Discipline
flashcards.content: Write down what you learned. Your memory will fail you.

activities.title: Read One Foundational Book in Your Field
activities.shortDescription: Read a classic or essential book that shapes your field
activities.objective: Build foundational knowledge that enriches all future learning
activities.motive: Foundational books teach you how to think, not just what to know

task.whatToDo: Read one foundational book central to your field or domain (e.g., 'The Lean Startup' for product, 'Sprint' for design).
task.howToDo: Read actively. Annotate. Discuss key ideas with colleagues. Apply one concept to your work.
task.suggestedStarter: I'm reading [Book] to understand the foundational thinking in [domain].
task.successCriteria: You completed the book and applied one key idea to a real project.

activities.reflectionPrompt: How does this book change how you think about your work?

flashcards.title: Impact
flashcards.content: Foundational books teach you frameworks, not just facts.

flashcards.title: Approach
flashcards.content: Read slowly. Annotate. Discuss. Apply.

activities.title: Teach a Workshop or Internal Training Session
activities.shortDescription: Share expertise through structured teaching
leadershipPrinciples: Success and Scale Bring Broad Responsibility
activities.objective: Scale your expertise and develop teaching skills
activities.motive: Teaching is learning at a higher level

task.whatToDo: Design and deliver a workshop or training on a skill or knowledge area where you have real expertise.
task.howToDo: Pick a topic. Outline lessons. Create materials. Run a 45–60 minute session. Gather feedback.
task.suggestedStarter: I want to share what I've learned about [topic] with the team. Here's my plan...
task.successCriteria: You delivered to 5+ people and they rated it as valuable and applicable.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What did you realize you didn't fully understand until you had to teach it?

flashcards.title: Mastery
flashcards.content: You can't teach what you don't deeply understand.

flashcards.title: Impact
flashcards.content: A 1-hour workshop scales your expertise to 20 people at once.

activities.title: Study Competitor or Industry Best Practices
activities.shortDescription: Learn how others solve the problems you're solving
activities.objective: Build perspective through external benchmarking
activities.motive: You learn faster from others' experiments than your own

task.whatToDo: Research how 3–5 competitors or industry leaders approach a challenge you're facing.
task.howToDo: Read case studies, articles, and documentation. Interview people who work there. Document what's different.
guidelines: Look for why they chose their approach
task.suggestedStarter: How do [competitors] solve [problem]? What can we learn without copying?
task.successCriteria: You identified 2–3 practices you could adapt and got buy-in to try one.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What do they do better, and what do we do better?

flashcards.title: Learning
flashcards.content: Best practices aren't stolen. They're learned and adapted.

flashcards.title: Discipline
flashcards.content: Study others to expand your thinking, not to panic about what you're missing.

activities.title: Complete an Advanced Course or Specialization
activities.shortDescription: Commit to deeper learning through a multi-month program
activities.objective: Build advanced capabilities in a critical area
activities.motive: Advanced learning separates practitioners from experts

task.whatToDo: Enroll in a multi-course specialization or advanced certificate program relevant to your growth path.
task.howToDo: Choose programs like Google Cloud certifications, AWS courses, or Coursera specializations. Complete all courses and capstone.
task.suggestedStarter: I'm pursuing the [Specialization] to develop expertise in [domain].
task.successCriteria: You completed all courses, passed assessments, and completed the capstone with distinction.

activities.reflectionPrompt: How does this advanced learning change what you can offer?

flashcards.title: Commitment
flashcards.content: Advanced learning requires consistent effort over months. It separates those who wish from those who do.

flashcards.title: Outcome
flashcards.content: Capstones matter. They prove you can apply what you learned.

activities.title: Interview Someone Who Solved a Problem You're Facing
activities.shortDescription: Learn through direct conversation with an expert
activities.objective: Access wisdom that's not in books or courses
activities.motive: Contextual learning from experts beats generic knowledge

task.whatToDo: Identify and interview someone who has successfully navigated a challenge you're currently facing.
task.howToDo: Prepare 5–7 questions. Ask about their approach, mistakes, and what they'd do differently. Take notes.
task.suggestedStarter: I know you've dealt with [challenge]. Would you share how you approached it? I'm facing it now.
task.successCriteria: You applied at least one insight from their experience to your situation.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What did they know that wasn't obvious?

flashcards.title: Value
flashcards.content: Direct conversations with experts are underrated learning tools.

flashcards.title: Approach
flashcards.content: Prepare questions. Ask permission. Take notes. Apply immediately.

activities.title: Build a Learning Dashboard or Knowledge Base
activities.shortDescription: Create a personal system for capturing and organizing what you learn
activities.objective: Make your learning retrievable and actionable
activities.motive: Knowledge you can't find is knowledge you'll relearn

task.whatToDo: Create a personal knowledge base (using Notion, Obsidian, or similar) to capture and organize what you learn.
task.howToDo: Organize by topic. Add insights, lessons, and references. Include what you've applied. Review monthly.
task.suggestedStarter: I'm building a knowledge base to capture what I'm learning so I can reference it later.
task.successCriteria: You added 10+ entries and referenced your knowledge base 3+ times for decision-making.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What connections did you make while organizing what you've learned?

flashcards.title: System
flashcards.content: A learning system turns ephemeral insights into permanent knowledge.

flashcards.title: Practice
flashcards.content: Capture → Organize → Review → Reference → Apply.

activities.title: Contribute to Open Source or a Public Project
activities.shortDescription: Learn by contributing to a real, public project
activities.objective: Learn through real contribution and peer feedback
activities.motive: Real projects teach you what courses never can

task.whatToDo: Contribute to an open source project, internal tool, or public knowledge base in your field.
task.howToDo: Start small. Fix bugs, add documentation, or solve small issues. Get feedback. Improve.
guidelines: Start with documentation, not complex code
task.suggestedStarter: I want to contribute to [project]. I'll start by [small task].
task.successCriteria: Your contribution was accepted and referenced by 2+ other users.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What did working with a real codebase or project teach you?

flashcards.title: Learning
flashcards.content: Real projects have real constraints. That's where real learning happens.

flashcards.title: Growth
flashcards.content: Open source feedback teaches you how others think.

activities.title: Create and Teach a Short Course or Learning Series
activities.shortDescription: Design multi-session learning experience for others
leadershipPrinciples: Success and Scale Bring Broad Responsibility
activities.objective: Scale knowledge transfer and develop advanced teaching skills
activities.motive: Teaching others deepens your own mastery exponentially

task.whatToDo: Design and deliver a 4–6 session learning series on a topic where you have significant expertise.
task.howToDo: Map curriculum. Create slides, exercises, resources. Deliver live with Q&A and feedback. Iterate.
task.suggestedStarter: I want to share what I've learned about [domain] through a 6-week learning series.
task.successCriteria: You completed all sessions with 8+ consistent participants who credited you with significant growth.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What did your students teach you by asking questions you hadn't considered?

flashcards.title: Impact
flashcards.content: A 6-week course reaches 10 people and changes their trajectory.

flashcards.title: Mastery
flashcards.content: Teaching complex ideas to beginners reveals gaps in your own understanding.

activities.title: Pursue a Micro-Credential or Specialized Nanodegree
activities.shortDescription: Complete a focused, accelerated advanced program
activities.objective: Build cutting-edge capability in an emerging area
activities.motive: Emerging skills command premium opportunity and impact

task.whatToDo: Complete a nanodegree or micro-credential in an emerging skill critical to your field (AI, advanced analytics, etc.).
task.howToDo: Choose platforms like Udacity. Complete projects with feedback. Build a portfolio piece.
guidelines: Focus on emerging, not established skills
task.suggestedStarter: I'm pursuing [micro-credential] in [emerging field] because it's going to matter significantly.
task.successCriteria: You completed with portfolio projects you can show and got hired or promoted based on it.

activities.reflectionPrompt: How will this emerging skill differentiate you in your field?

flashcards.title: Advantage
flashcards.content: Emerging skills have less competition and higher opportunity.

flashcards.title: Investment
flashcards.content: 3 months of learning in an emerging field can change your trajectory.

activities.title: Publish Research or Conduct an Experiment
activities.shortDescription: Generate new knowledge through systematic investigation
activities.objective: Move from learning to knowledge creation
activities.motive: Creating knowledge compounds your learning exponentially

task.whatToDo: Design and conduct a work-related experiment or research project. Document and share findings.
task.howToDo: Form a hypothesis. Design method. Collect data. Analyze. Write up findings. Share with peers.
task.suggestedStarter: I've been wondering if [hypothesis] is true. Here's how I tested it...
task.successCriteria: You published findings and at least 5 people referenced your research in their decisions.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What did the actual data teach you that you suspected?

flashcards.title: Shift
flashcards.content: From 'what do I learn' to 'what do I know that others don't.'

flashcards.title: Method
flashcards.content: Hypothesis → Design → Data → Analysis → Publish.

activities.title: Become a Thought Leader in Your Niche
activities.shortDescription: Build public expertise and influence through consistent contribution
leadershipPrinciples: Success and Scale Bring Broad Responsibility
activities.objective: Establish yourself as a recognized authority
activities.motive: Thought leadership amplifies your impact across organizations

task.whatToDo: Establish yourself as a recognized expert through writing, speaking, and community contribution.
task.howToDo: Write monthly articles. Speak at 2–3 events. Contribute to key community conversations. Build audience.
guidelines: Back up claims with data or experience
task.suggestedStarter: I want to become known for my thinking on [niche]. Here's my platform plan...
task.successCriteria: You have 500+ people following your work and receive 3+ speaking invitations annually.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What's your unique perspective that others need to hear?

flashcards.title: Authority
flashcards.content: Thought leadership = Consistent value + Unique perspective + Real proof.

flashcards.title: Growth
flashcards.content: Monthly writing beats sporadic brilliance.

activities.title: Complete a Master's Degree or Advanced Credential
activities.shortDescription: Invest in formal, comprehensive advanced education
activities.objective: Build comprehensive, accredited expertise
activities.motive: Advanced degrees open doors and teach you how to learn at the deepest level

task.whatToDo: Pursue a relevant Master's degree, MBA, or advanced credential in your field or a growth area.
task.howToDo: Research programs. Apply strategically. Commit fully. Network with classmates. Finish strong.
task.suggestedStarter: I'm pursuing my [degree] to deepen expertise in [domain] and access better opportunities.
task.successCriteria: You graduated, built meaningful relationships, and it directly influenced your career trajectory.

activities.reflectionPrompt: How did this degree change your thinking about your field?

flashcards.title: Investment
flashcards.content: A relevant advanced degree is both personal growth and career investment.

flashcards.title: Value
flashcards.content: The degree matters. The network matters more.

activities.title: Lead a Cross-Functional Learning Initiative
activities.shortDescription: Design learning experience that spans departments or organizations
leadershipPrinciples: Success and Scale Bring Broad Responsibility
activities.objective: Create organizational learning infrastructure
activities.motive: Organizational learning multiplies individual learning

task.whatToDo: Design and lead a learning initiative that spans departments (e.g., lunch & learns series, learning circles, peer education program).
task.howToDo: Identify topic. Recruit facilitators. Schedule series. Track engagement. Measure impact.
task.suggestedStarter: I want to start a [learning initiative] that brings different teams together to learn about [topic].
task.successCriteria: You sustained the program with 20+ consistent participants and it influenced company practice.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What surprised you about how people learn together?

flashcards.title: Scale
flashcards.content: Cross-functional learning breaks silos and creates organizational learning.

flashcards.title: Leadership
flashcards.content: Initiative → Facilitation → Community → Sustained Impact.

activities.title: Create a Comprehensive Resource Library or Curriculum
activities.shortDescription: Build a lasting learning resource for your domain
leadershipPrinciples: Success and Scale Bring Broad Responsibility
activities.objective: Codify expertise into transferable knowledge
activities.motive: Resources you create teach long after you're gone

task.whatToDo: Create a comprehensive learning resource library or curriculum for a skill or domain (internal docs, guides, video series).
task.howToDo: Outline scope. Gather resources. Organize. Create original content. Get feedback. Maintain.
guidelines: Make it complete for beginners to intermediate
task.suggestedStarter: I'm creating a comprehensive resource library for [topic] so people don't have to figure it out like I did.
task.successCriteria: Your resource is the standard reference in your organization with 50+ monthly users.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What gaps did you fill that nothing else addresses?

flashcards.title: Legacy
flashcards.content: A well-built resource library teaches hundreds without your direct involvement.

flashcards.title: Structure
flashcards.content: Outline → Resources → Organization → Original content → Feedback → Maintenance.

activities.title: Build an Expert Network and Stay Current
activities.shortDescription: Develop relationships with 5+ leading experts in your field
activities.objective: Create access to cutting-edge thinking through direct relationships
activities.motive: Networks keep you at the cutting edge faster than any course

task.whatToDo: Identify and build relationships with 5+ recognized experts or thought leaders in your domain.
task.howToDo: Connect authentically. Share their work. Engage thoughtfully. Offer value. Meet in person when possible.
task.suggestedStarter: I've been following your work on [topic]. I'd love to grab coffee and learn how you think about it.
task.successCriteria: You have ongoing relationships with 5+ experts and regularly get early insights before the public.

activities.reflectionPrompt: How has direct access to experts changed your perspective?

flashcards.title: Access
flashcards.content: Your network determines your access to cutting-edge thinking.

flashcards.title: Approach
flashcards.content: Authentic engagement → Shared interests → Real relationships → Early insights.

activities.title: Conduct Industry Research and Publish Findings
activities.shortDescription: Create original research that shapes industry thinking
activities.objective: Move from thought leader to research leader
activities.motive: Research amplifies thought leadership into industry influence

task.whatToDo: Conduct original industry or domain research. Publish findings in reputable venues.
task.howToDo: Identify research question. Design study. Collect data. Analyze. Write report. Publish. Present findings.
task.suggestedStarter: I've been wondering about [industry question]. I'm conducting research to answer it rigorously.
task.successCriteria: Your research was published and cited by 10+ articles or influenced industry conversation.

activities.reflectionPrompt: How did conducting research change what you thought you knew?

flashcards.title: Authority
flashcards.content: Research-backed thinking beats opinion-based thinking every time.

flashcards.title: Process
flashcards.content: Question → Design → Data → Analysis → Publish → Present.

activities.title: Establish a Learning Council or Advisory Board
activities.shortDescription: Create institutional structure for ongoing learning and development
leadershipPrinciples: Success and Scale Bring Broad Responsibility
activities.objective: Build learning into organizational fabric
activities.motive: Learning councils ensure learning survives leadership transitions

task.whatToDo: Establish a learning council or advisory board that guides organizational learning strategy.
task.howToDo: Recruit diverse representatives. Set mission. Meet regularly. Allocate budget. Measure impact.
task.suggestedStarter: I want to establish a learning council that guides how we invest in professional development.
task.successCriteria: Council meets quarterly for 12+ months and makes decisions that shape organizational learning.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What did collective wisdom reveal that individual thinking missed?

flashcards.title: Institution
flashcards.content: Learning councils make learning an organizational priority, not an afterthought.

flashcards.title: Structure
flashcards.content: Recruitment → Mission → Cadence → Budget → Outcomes.

activities.title: Mentor the Next Generation of Experts in Your Field
activities.shortDescription: Build the future of your domain through deliberate mentoring
leadershipPrinciples: Success and Scale Bring Broad Responsibility
activities.objective: Shape the field's future through mentoring early-career professionals
activities.motive: Your mentees will do things you never will

task.whatToDo: Take on 3+ junior professionals as mentees, with the explicit goal of helping them become experts in your field.
task.howToDo: Meet regularly. Expose them to challenges. Build their network. Open doors. Hold them accountable.
guidelines: Vary your mentees' backgrounds and perspectives
task.suggestedStarter: I'm mentoring the next generation in [field] because I want to shape where this domain goes.
task.successCriteria: At least 2 of your mentees have advanced significantly in the field and attribute it to your mentoring.

activities.reflectionPrompt: What are your mentees teaching you?

flashcards.title: Legacy
flashcards.content: Your impact multiplies through the experts you develop.

flashcards.title: Approach
flashcards.content: Exposure → Challenge → Network → Accountability → Advancement.

activities.title: Shape Industry Standards or Direction
activities.shortDescription: Influence how your entire field approaches learning and practice
leadershipPrinciples: Success and Scale Bring Broad Responsibility
activities.objective: Move from individual expertise to field-level influence
activities.motive: Shaping standards multiplies your impact across the entire profession

task.whatToDo: Take a leadership role in shaping industry standards, best practices, or curriculum in your field.
task.howToDo: Join standards bodies, work with associations, contribute to curriculum development, publish authoritative guides.
task.suggestedStarter: I want to help shape how [industry/field] thinks about [critical area].
task.successCriteria: You contributed to standards or practices adopted by 50+ organizations in your field.

activities.reflectionPrompt: How will professionals practice differently because of your contribution?

flashcards.title: Impact
flashcards.content: Shaping standards changes how thousands practice their profession.

flashcards.title: Responsibility
flashcards.content: With influence comes responsibility to get it right.
